headline: Managua starts writing the electoral wall
deck: Nicaragua’s legislature and electoral council are turning Daniel Ortega’s order into law. The project preserves voting while deciding which opponents may reach the ballot.
body: Nicaragua’s constitutional commission met Supreme Electoral Council magistrates on Wednesday to prepare changes ordered by co-president Daniel Ortega. Assembly president Gustavo Porras said the work would prevent people the government calls coup plotters and traitors from using elections to take power. The commission plans consultations before seeking a first vote in early August. [E1][E2]

The distinction between ending elections and ending competition matters. Ortega’s 19 July speech was widely reported as a promise of “no more elections,” but the Assembly’s account still describes future elections. Its proposed wall is aimed at opposition figures, foreign-supported organizations and candidates promoted through media campaigns. Ballots remain inside the official frame; viable challengers may not. [E1][E2][E4]

Much of the machinery is already in place. Constitutional changes published in 2025 established a co-presidency, extended the presidential term to six years and allowed the disqualification of people accused of leading or financing a coup. They also subordinated state bodies more closely to the presidency. [E3]

Wednesday’s meeting shifts the project from a rally speech to an institutional timetable. The constitutional commission and electoral magistrates are preparing matrices, consulting state bodies and discussing changes to the electoral authority’s functions. No draft language has been published, so the breadth of any exclusion power remains unknown. [E2]

Officials present the reforms as protection against foreign intervention and a repeat of the 2018 unrest. That claim does not answer the central design question: who decides that a party is foreign-directed or that a candidate is a traitor, what evidence is required, and whether a court can reverse the finding. Broad labels administered by a presidency-aligned electoral council would make exclusion a routine procedure rather than an exceptional sanction. [E1][E2][E3]

A narrower law could target undeclared foreign financing while leaving plural competition intact. The government’s language points further. Porras described a barrier against whole political categories, and Ortega said opponents would not regain power through elections. Publication of the bill will reveal whether the rhetoric becomes a discretionary veto or another layer on existing restrictions. [E1][E4][E5]

The first test is documentary, not ceremonial. A committee vote without text would show obedience; a gazetted provision naming prohibited actors and assigning the council enforcement power would show the wall’s legal shape. Until then, Nicaragua has announced the destination and begun pouring foundations without publishing the plans. [E1][E2]

evidence_box.fragment: cuándo y cómo serán nuestras elecciones
Constitución Política de la República de Nicaragua
Ortega says Nicaragua will no longer hold elections

art.title: The vote from Managua
art.caption: The constitutional commission and electoral council are drafting the next set of restrictions in Managua · Map: Tinkerton, Policy Desk · Terrain: NOAA ETOPO1
